content: The area enclosed between the curve y = 1 + x2, the y-axis, and the straight line y = 5 is given by
explanation:
Given equation of curve is y = 1 + x2 or x2 = (y $$-$$ 1)

This is equation of parabola having vertex (0, 1)

$$\therefore$$ Required area $$ = \int\limits_1^5 {\sqrt {y - 1} \,dy} $$

$$ = {2 \over 3}\left[ {{{(y - 1)}^{3/2}}} \right]_1^5$$

$$ = {2 \over 3}\left[ {{{(4)}^{3/2}}} \right] = {2 \over 3} \times 8 = {{16} \over 3}$$ sq. units

content: The line which is parallel to x-axis and crosses the curve y = $$\sqrt x$$ at an angle 45$$^\circ$$ is
explanation:
Since equation of any line parallel to y-axis is y = k where k is any real constant. We draw a tangent which makes an angle 45$$^\circ$$ with the line parallel to x-axis.

Given equation of curve is

$$y = \sqrt x $$ ..... (1)

Taking derivative

$${{dy} \over {dx}} = {1 \over {2\sqrt x }}$$

Since $${{dy} \over {dx}} = \tan 45^\circ = 1$$

$$\therefore$$ $${1 \over {2\sqrt x }} = 1$$ or $$x = {1 \over 4}$$

Substituting $$x = {1 \over 4}$$ in (1)

$$y = \sqrt {{1 \over 4}} = {1 \over 2}$$

$$\therefore$$ Point $$P\left( {{1 \over 4},{1 \over 2}} \right)$$, it also passes through the line parallel to x-axis.

$$\therefore$$ $$y = {1 \over 2}$$ is required equation of line.

content: The area included between the parabolas y2 = 4x and x2 = 4y is
explanation:
Equation of curves are

y2 = 4x ..... (i)

and x2 = 4y .... (ii)

From (i) and (ii)

$${\left( {{{{x^2}} \over 4}} \right)^2} = 4x \Rightarrow {x^4} - 64x = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow x({x^3} - 64) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0$$ or $$x = 4$$

$$\therefore$$ Required area $$ = \int\limits_0^4 {\left( {\sqrt {4x} - {{{x^2}} \over 4}} \right)dx = \left[ {2.{{2{x^{3/2}}} \over 3} - {{{x^3}} \over {12}}} \right]_0^4} $$

$$ = {{32} \over 3} - {{64} \over {12}} = {{32} \over 3} - {{16} \over 3} = {{16} \over 3}$$ sq. unit.

content: The area enclosed by y = 3x $$-$$ 5, y = 0, x = 3 and x = 5 is
explanation:
$$\because$$ y = 3x $$-$$ 5

$$\therefore$$ $${x \over {\left( {{5 \over 3}} \right)}} + {y \over { - 5}} = 1$$

$$\therefore$$ Required area ABCD

$$ = \int\limits_3^5 {y\,.\,dx = \int\limits_3^5 {(3x - 5)dx} } $$

$$ = \left[ {3\left( {{{{x^2}} \over 2}} \right) - 5x} \right]_3^5 = {3 \over 2}({5^2} - {3^2}) - 5(5 - 3)$$

$$ = 24 - 10 = 14$$ sq. units

content: The area bounded by the parabolas y = 4x2, $$y = {{{x^2}} \over 9}$$ and the line y = 2 is
explanation:
$${x^2} = {1 \over 4}y$$

$${x^2} = 9y$$

Required area = 2 $$\times$$ Area OABO

$$ = 2\int\limits_0^2 {({x_2} - {x_1})dy} $$ [where x1, x2 are values of x from (i) and (ii) respectively]

$$ = 2\int\limits_0^2 {\left( {3\sqrt y - {1 \over 2}\sqrt y } \right)dy = 2\left( {{5 \over 2}} \right)\int\limits_0^2 {{y^{{1 \over 2}}}dy} } $$

$$ = 5{\left( {{2 \over 3}} \right){\left[ {y ^{3 \over 2}} \right]_0^2}} = {{10} \over 3}\left( {{2^{{3 \over 2}}} - 0} \right) = {{20\sqrt 2 } \over 3}$$ sq. units

content: The area of the region bounded by y2 = x and y = | x | is
explanation:
y2 = x .... (1)

and y = | x | ..... (2)

$$ \Rightarrow y = \left\{ {\matrix{ {x,} & {x > 0} \cr { - x,} & {x < 0} \cr } } \right.$$

On solving (1) & (2), x = 0, 1

$$\therefore$$ Required area OQBPO = Area (OABPO) $$-$$ Area (OABQO)

$$ = \int\limits_0^1 {\sqrt x dx - \int\limits_0^1 {x\,.\,dx = \left[ {{2 \over 3}{x^{3/2}} - {{{x^2}} \over 2}} \right]_0^1 = {2 \over 3} - {1 \over 2} = {1 \over 6}} } $$

content: The area enclosed between y2 = x and y = x is
explanation:
$$\because$$ On solving we get x = 0, 1

$$\therefore$$ Required area OQBPO

= Area OABPO $$-$$ Area OABQO

$$ = \int\limits_0^1 {\sqrt x dx - \int\limits_0^1 {x\,.\,dx = \left[ {{2 \over 3}{x^{3/2}} - {{{x^2}} \over 2}} \right]_0^1} } $$

$$ = {2 \over 3} - {1 \over 2} = {1 \over 6}$$ sq. units.

content: The area bounded by y2 = 4x and x2 = 4y is
explanation:
y2 = 4x ...... (i)

x2 = 4y ..... (ii)

On solving, x = 0, 4

$$\therefore$$ Required area OQBPO = Area OABPO $$-$$ Area OABQO

$$ = \int\limits_0^4 {\sqrt {4x} dx - \int\limits_0^4 {{{{x^2}} \over 4}\,.\,dx = \left[ {2\,.\,{2 \over 3}{x^{3/2}} - {1 \over 4}\,.\,{{{x^3}} \over 3}} \right]_0^4} } $$

$$ = {4 \over 3}\,.\,{4^{3/2}} - {1 \over {12}}\,.\,{4^3} = {{32} \over 3} - {{16} \over 3} = {{16} \over 3}$$ sq. units

content: The area bounded by the curves $$x=4-y^2$$ and the Y-axis is
explanation:
$x=4-y^2$ and $y$-axis

$y^2=4-x \Rightarrow$ Parabola

$\begin{aligned} & \text { Required area }=2 \int_0^2\left(4-y^2\right) d y=2\left[4 y-\frac{y^3}{3}\right]_0^2 \\\\ & =2\left[\left(4.2-\frac{2^3}{3}\right)-(0)\right]=\frac{32}{3} \text { sq. unit }\end{aligned}$

content: Consider the function $$\mathrm{f}(x)=(x-2) \log _{\mathrm{e}} x$$. Then the equation $$x \log _{\mathrm{e}} x=2-x$$

options.content: has at least one root in $$(1,2)$$

content: Area of the figure bounded by the parabola $${y^2} + 8x = 16$$ and $${y^2} - 24x = 48$$ is

Let f be a non-negative function defined in $$[0,\pi /2]$$, f' exists and be continuous for all x and $$\int\limits_0^x {\sqrt {1 - {{(f'(t))}^2}} dt = \int\limits_0^x {f(t)dt} } $$ and f (0) = 0. Then
explanation:
Given, $$\int\limits_0^x {\sqrt {1 - {{(f'(t))}^2}} dt = \int\limits_0^x {f(t)\,dt,0 \le x \le {\pi \over 2}} } $$

On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x by using Leibnitz rule, we get

$$\sqrt {1 - {{[f'(x)]}^2}} = f(x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow f'(x) = \, \pm \,\sqrt {1 - {{[f(x)]}^2}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow \int {{{f'(x)} \over {\sqrt {1 - {{[f(x)]}^2}} }}dx = \, \pm \,\int {dx} } $$

$$ \Rightarrow {\sin ^{ - 1}}[f(x)] = \, \pm \,x + c$$

Put $$x = 0 \Rightarrow {\sin ^{ - 1}}[f(0)] = \pm \,0 + c$$

$$ \Rightarrow c = {\sin ^{ - 1}}(0) = 0$$ [$$\because$$ $$f(0) = 0$$]

$$\therefore$$ $$f(x) = \pm \,\sin x$$

$$\because$$ f is a non-negative function defined in $$\left[ {0,{\pi \over 2}} \right]$$.

$$\therefore$$ $$f(x) = \sin x$$

And we know that, $$\sin x < x,\forall x > 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\sin \left( {{1 \over 2}} \right) < {1 \over 2}$$ and $$\sin \left( {{1 \over 3}} \right) < {1 \over 3} \Rightarrow f\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right) < {1 \over 2}$$

and $$f\left( {{1 \over 3}} \right) < {1 \over 3}$$

Similarity $$f\left( {{4 \over 3}} \right) < {4 \over 3}$$ and $$f\left( {{2 \over 3}} \right) < {2 \over 3}$$

content: The straight the through the origin which divides the area formed by the curves y = 2x $$-$$ x<sup>2</sup>, y = 0 and x = 1 into two equal halves is
explanation:
Required area

$$ = \int\limits_0^1 {(2x - {x^2})dx} $$

$$ = \left[ {2.{{{x^2}} \over 2} - {{{x^3}} \over 3}} \right]_0^1$$

$$ = \left[ {{x^2} - {{{x^3}} \over 3}} \right]_0^1$$

$$ = \left( {1 - {1 \over 3}} \right)$$

$$ = {2 \over 3}$$

Let, the equation of line passes through the origin is y = mx.

This line and x = 1 line cut at point B.

y = m . 1 = m

$$\therefore$$ Point B = (1, m)

Area of $$\Delta OAB = {1 \over 2} \times OA \times AB$$

$$ = {1 \over 2} \times 1 \times m$$

Given that straight line cut the area two equal parts.

$$\therefore$$ 2 $$\times$$ Area of $$\Delta OAB = {2 \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2 \times {1 \over 2} \times 1 \times m = {2 \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow m = {2 \over 3}$$

$$\therefore$$ Equation of straight line,

$$y = {2 \over 3}x$$

content: The area bounded by the parabolas $$y = 4{x^2},y = {{{x^2}} \over 9}$$ and the straight line y = 2 is
explanation:
Given curve, $$y = 4{x^2},y = {{{x^2}} \over 9}$$ and y = 2

Graph of given curve


Area of shaded region

$$ = 2\int_0^2 {\left( {3\sqrt y - {{\sqrt y } \over 2}} \right)dy} $$

$$ = 2\int_2^0 {{{5\sqrt y } \over 2}dy} $$

$$ = 5 \times {2 \over 3}[{y^{3/2}}]_0^2$$

$$ = {{10} \over 3}[{2^{3/2}} - 0]$$

$$ = {{10} \over 3} \times 2\sqrt 2 $$

$$ = {{20} \over 3}\sqrt 2 $$ sq. unit

content: If $${x^2} + {y^2} = {a^2}$$, then $$\int\limits_0^a {\sqrt {1 + {{\left( {{{dy} \over {dx}}} \right)}^2}} dx = } $$
explanation:
Given, x2 + y2 = a2

On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,

$$2x + 2y{{dy} \over {dx}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{dy} \over {dx}} = {{ - x} \over y}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\left( {{{dy} \over {dx}}} \right)^2} = {{{x^2}} \over {{y^2}}}$$

Let $$I = \int\limits_0^a {\sqrt {1 + {{\left( {{{dy} \over {dx}}} \right)}^2}} dx} $$

$$I = \int\limits_0^a {\sqrt {1 + {{{x^2}} \over {{y^2}}}} dx} $$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$I = \int\limits_0^a {\sqrt {{{{x^2} + {y^2}} \over {{y^2}}}} dx} $$

$$I = \int\limits_0^a {{a \over {\sqrt {{a^2} - {x^2}} }}dx} $$ [$$ \because $$ x2 + y2 = a2]

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$I = a\left[ {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}{x \over a}} \right]_0^a$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$I = a\left[ {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}{a \over a} - {{\sin }^{ - 1}}0} \right]$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$I = a\left[ {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}1 - {{\sin }^{ - 1}}0} \right]$$

= $$a\left[ {{\pi \over 2} - 0} \right] = {\pi \over 2}a$$

content: Area in the first quadrant between the ellipses x<sup>2</sup> + 2y<sup>2</sup> = a<sup>2</sup> and 2x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> = a<sup>2</sup> is
explanation:
Given curve

x2 + 2y2 = a2 and 2x2 + y2 = a2


$$ \therefore $$ Required area = $$\left( {{{{a^2}} \over 6} + \int_{a/\sqrt 3 }^{a/\sqrt 2 } {\sqrt {{a^2} - 2{x^2}} dx} } \right) \times 2$$

$$ = {{{a^2}} \over {\sqrt 2 }}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right)$$

content:
The area of the region

$$\{ (x,y):{x^2} + {y^2} \le 1 \le x + y\} $$ is
explanation:
Area of region

$$\{ (x,y):{x^2} + {y^2} \le 1 \le x + y\} $$

$$ \therefore $$ x2 + y2 = 1 and x + y = 1


Area of shaded region = Area of quadrant $$ - $$ Area of triangle

$$ = {\pi \over 4}{(1)^2} - {1 \over 2} \times 1 \times 1$$

= $${\pi \over 4} - {1 \over 2}$$

content:
Let P and T be the subsets of k, y-plane defined by

P = {(x, y) : x > 0, y > 0 and x2 + y2 = 1}

T = {(x, y) : x > 0, y > 0 and x8 + y8 < 1}

Then, P $$ \cap $$ T is
explanation:
From the graph, it is clear that there is common region which satisfies x2 + y2 = 1 and x8 + y8 < 1

$$ \because $$ P $$ \cap $$ T = P

content: The area of the figure bounded by the parabolas x = $$-$$ 2y<sup>2</sup> and x = 1 $$-$$ 3y<sup>2</sup> is
explanation:
We have,

$$x = - 2{y^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {y^2} = - {1 \over 2}x$$ .... (i)

and $$x = 1 - 3{y^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {y^2} = - {1 \over 3}(x - 1)$$ .... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$ \Rightarrow {y^2} = - {1 \over 3}(x - 1)$$

$$ - {1 \over 2}x = - {1 \over 3}(x - 1)$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3x = 2(x - 1)$$

$$ \Rightarrow 3x = 2x - 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = - 2$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${y^2} = - {1 \over 2}( - 1) = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow y = \pm 1$$

$$ \therefore $$ Point of intersection of two curves is ($$-$$ 2, $$ \pm $$ 1)


$$ \therefore $$ Required Area = $$2\int_0^1 {\sin x\,dx} $$

$$ = 2\left[ {y - {{{y^3}} \over 3}} \right]_0^1$$

$$ = 2\left[ {1 - {1 \over 3}} \right]$$

$$ = {4 \over 3}$$ sq units

content: The area of the figure bounded by the parabola $$x =  - 2{y^2},\,x = 1 - 3{y^2}$$ is
explanation:
Intersecting points of both curves are $$P( - 2,1)$$ and $$Q( - 2, - 1)$$.


$$ \therefore $$ Area of the bounded region

$$ = \int_{ - 1}^1 {((1 - 3{y^2}) - ( - 2{y^2}))\,dy} $$

$$ = 2\int_0^1 {(1 - {y^2})\,dy} $$

$$ = 2\left( {y - {{{y^3}} \over 3}} \right)_0^1$$

$$ = 2\left( {1 - {1 \over 3}} \right) = {4 \over 3}$$ sq unit

content: The area bounded by y = x + 1 and y = cos x and the X-axis, is
explanation:
We have,

y = x + 1 and y = cos x


$$ \therefore $$ Required area = $$\int_{ - 1}^0 {(x + 1)dx + \int_0^{\pi /2} {\cos xdx} } $$

$$ = \left[ {{{{x^2}} \over 2} + x} \right]_1^0 + [\sin x]_0^{\pi /2}$$

$$ = \left[ {(0) - \left( {{1 \over 2} - 1} \right)} \right] + [1 - 0]$$

$$ = {1 \over 2} + 1 = {3 \over 2}$$ sq unit.
